coredata.dc:description: Recent progress in the co-spinning of nanofibers and semiconductor particles offers a promising strategy for the development of photocatalytic devices, solving aggregation and catalyst recovery challenges. However, composite photocatalysts based on nanofiber membranes often suffer from poor conductivity, low hydrophilicity, and easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the semiconductor components. Here, to tackle the aforementioned issues of ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (ZIS/PAN) nanofiber-based catalysts, we prepared a composite carbon dots/ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (CZP) nanofiber membrane by blending carbon dots (CDs) with ZIS/PAN using the electrospinning process. The hydrogen evolution performance of the CZP photocatalyst was significantly improved by CDs, which enhanced the hydrophilicity, increased the light absorption, facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and reduced the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Notably, the optimal CZP photocatalyst achieved a hydrogen evolution rate of 2250 μmol g-1h−1, which is about 23 % higher than that of the nanofiber membrane without CDs and 4.55 times higher than that of ZIS particles. The present work successfully improved the CZP nanofiber membrane of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance, and the membrane may benefit further device development by eliminating the need for stirring and simplifying the recovery process.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional CDs doping enhances the properties of CDs/ZIS/PAN nanofiber membrane: increased the hydrophilicity, promoted the light absorption, facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electrons.

Recent progress in the co-spinning of nanofibers and semiconductor particles offers a promising strategy for the development of photocatalytic devices, solving aggregation and catalyst recovery challenges. However, composite photocatalysts based on nanofiber membranes often suffer from poor conductivity, low hydrophilicity, and easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the semiconductor components. Here, to tackle the aforementioned issues of ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (ZIS/PAN) nanofiber-based catalysts, we prepared a composite carbon dots/ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (CZP) nanofiber membrane by blending carbon dots (CDs) with ZIS/PAN using the electrospinning process. The hydrogen evolution performance of the CZP photocatalyst was significantly improved by CDs, which enhanced the hydrophilicity, increased the light absorption, facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and reduced the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Notably, the optimal CZP photocatalyst achieved a hydrogen evolution rate of 2250 μmol g-1h−1, which is about 23 % higher than that of the nanofiber membrane without CDs and 4.55 times higher than that of ZIS particles. The present work successfully improved the CZP nanofiber membrane of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance, and the membrane may benefit further device development by eliminating the need for stirring and simplifying the recovery process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the field of photocatalysis, enhancing the catalytic efficiency of photocatalysts has been attracting significant attention. ZnIn2S4 (ZIS), a ternary metal sulfide semiconductor photocatalyst, possesses an appropriate band gap, good visible light response, and environmentally friendly characteristics, commonly used in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [1]. However, when ZIS powder is directly applied in photocatalysis, it often suffers from low light utilization efficiency [2], easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [3], and severe powder agglomeration [4], which limits its practical application. Previous research [5] has focused on solving these challenges by developing composite photocatalysts based on nanofiber membranes. The Three-dimensional nanofiber membranes using electrospinning technology have a large specific surface area [6] and small fiber diameters. Moreover, the membrane can adsorb byproducts generated during photocatalysis [7], making it easy to recycle. This method effectively alleviates the complex recovery process [8] and high cost [9] of traditional photocatalyst powder, with cycle stability, which is expected to be put into practical application [10–12].

While previous work has successfully demonstrated the concept for composite photocatalysts based on nanofiber membranes, further advancements are essential for the practical application in photocatalysis. The focus should now shift towards achieving higher performance metrics. Several critical issues require to be resolved: the poor hydrophilicity [13,14] and the low light absorption performance [15–17] of the nanofiber membrane, as well as the propensity for photogenerated electron-hole recombination in ZIS photocatalysts [18,19]. Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based material with sizes less than 10 nm [20,21], possess numerous advantageous properties such as low toxicity, water solubility, electrical conductivity, photosensitivity, and optical stability [22]. These attributes render CDs highly impactful in the field of photocatalysis, even in minimal quantities [23]. Notably, CDs exhibit excellent hydrophilicity [24], diverse light absorption mechanisms, and quantum effects that contribute to superior optical properties [25]. In photocatalytic applications, their ability to capture electrons and facilitate electron transfer plays a crucial role in enhancing the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [26,27]. Integrating CDs into the photocatalytic system can effectively overcome the limitations identified in previous studies. Their excellent hydrophilicity, superior light absorption capabilities, and ability to prevent the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers provide a pathway to significantly improve photocatalytic efficiency.

In this study, we synthesized a carbon dots/ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (CZP) nanofiber membrane by blending CDs and ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (ZIS/PAN) through electrospinning. This composite membrane demonstrates excellent recyclability and cycling stability, highlighting the versatility and feasibility of integrating nanofibers with semiconductor particles. The hydrogen evolution performance of the CZP photocatalyst is improved by CDs significantly, which enhances the hydrophilicity, increases light absorption, facilitates the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and reduces the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. These improvements enable efficient charge transfer and separation, leading to a substantial enhancement in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity and photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance. Notably, the optimal CZP photocatalyst achieved a hydrogen evolution rate of 2250 μmol g-1h−1, which is about 23 % higher than that of the nanofiber membrane without CDs and 4.55 times higher than that of ZIS particles. The addition of CDs, even in minimal quantities of less than 1 %, markedly boosts the photocatalytic activity, underscoring the design flexibility and potential diversity of fiber-photocatalyst composites. This advancement provides a valuable option for the further development and deviceization of photocatalysts.

N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), ethyl alcohol (EtOH, 99.8 %), indium trichloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O, 99.99 %), thioacetamide (TAA, 99 %), triethanolamine (TEOA, 99 %) and anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, Mw = 150000) was obtained from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zinc chloride (ZnCl2) was purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6) and ferrocene (Fe(C5H5)2) were purchased from Sigma Odrich Shanghai Trading Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Synthesis of CDs. CDs were produced by a typical electrolytic method using two graphite sticks as a carbon source [28]. Firstly, the graphite rods were ultrasonically cleaned in ultrapure water for 30 mins to remove impurities on the surface. Then, the two graphite rods were used as anode and cathode respectively in an electrolytic bath of ultrapure water for half a month, until the colorless electrolyte changed to a brownish-black solution. Afterward, the solution was filtered and centrifuged to remove the big graphite particles. Finally, the pure CDs solution was obtained.

Synthesis of ZIS . The ZIS particles were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction method (Fig. S1) [29]. ZnCl2 (0.136 g), TAA (0.3 g), and InCl3·H2O (0.586 g) were added to a 60 mL EtOH and stirred vigorously for 1.5h. The solution was transferred into a stainless steel autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 ℃ for 5 h. The resultant product was collected by centrifugation and subsequently washed with ethanol and deionized water. After washing, the product was dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

Preparation of CZP spinning solution. Five samples of PAN (0.665 g) were respectively dissolved in the pure CDs solution (at a certain volume of 0.625, 1.25, 1.875, 2.5, 3.125 mL) at 60 ℃ in the oven for 12 h, and then five various ratios CDs/PAN powder were obtained. DMF (6.3 g) was mixed with CDs/PAN powder by magnetic stirring at room temperature for 3 h to get a homogenous dark solution. The solution changed to a uniform sea green solution after ZIS (0.035 g) was added into CDs/PAN/DMF solution by magnetic stirring at room temperature for 9 h. Accordingly, the fabricated five different proportions of CZP spinning solution were designated as CZP-1, CZP-2, CZP-3, CZP-4, and CZP-5, respectively.

Electrospinning. Electrospinning of the five different proportions of CDs solution (CZP-1, CZP-2, CZP-3, CZP-4, CZP-5) was carried out with a high voltage of 20 kV. Electrospinning parameters used were a pumping rate of 1 mL h−1 for 5 h, a collecting roller speed of 500 rpm, and a 150 mm distance between the needle and the grounded collection plate. The ambient temperature was (30 ± 5) ℃, and the relative humidity was (50 ± 5) %. After all, these samples were dried for six hours at 60 ℃ in a vacuum drying oven.

The field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, S-4700, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 3 kV, the desktop scanning electron microscope (SEM, TM3030, Hitachi, Japan) with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) analysis at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV, and the transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA) were used to characterize the morphology and structure of the samples. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were collected at room temperature using a Fourier intelligent infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 5700, Thermo Electron, USA) with 16 acquisitions, a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 and a spectral acquisition range of 4000–500 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) was carried out with the range from 10.0 to 60.0° at a scan rate of 0.15° s−1 under a recording interval of 0.04°. The mechanical properties of the samples (40 mm × 10 mm) were tested by a double-arm material testing machine (INSTRON-3365, INSTRON, USA) with a clamping distance of 20 mm and a tensile rate of 10 mm min−1. A contact angle measuring device (DSA100S, KRÜSS GmbH, Germany) was used to measure the water contact angle in the air with a water droplet volume of 1 μL. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) conducted on a Thermogravimetric differential thermal analyzer (TG/DTA7300, Nippon Seiko Nano Technology Co, Japan) was used to measure the decomposing temperature of samples. The measurement was conducted from 30 to 800 ℃ under a nitrogen environment with a ramp rate of 20 K min−1. The absorption of samples to different wavelengths of light and the band gap was measured by an ultraviolet–visible near-infrared spectrometer (UV–Vis, UV3600, Shimadzu, Japan) with a medium scanning speed and a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. A transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (PL, FLS920, Edinburgh, UK) was applied to characterize the samples at a certain excitation wavelength of 320 nm with a slit width of 1 nm. Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) were conducted using an Edinburgh Instruments Ltd FLS980 with excitation and emission wavelengths of 320 and 470 nm, respectively. XploRA plus Raman spectroscopy (Jobin Yvon, France) was employed to qualitatively investigate the molecular structure of the samples with an excitation light source of 532 nm and a wavelength range of 100–3000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was used to determine the chemical composition of the surface of the CZP nanofiber membranes and ZIS powder as well as the valence state information. All of the spectra were referenced to the C1s at the binding energy of 284.8 eV.

Using a three-electrode system made up of a working electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode, and a reference electrode, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent response test (i-t), Mott-Schottky test (M−S), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were performed in the photoelectrochemical measurement on an electrochemical station (CHI 760E, Chenhua, Shanghai). Especially, the CV samples were added to the Nafion/EtOH solution for ultrasonic dispersion for 0.5 h, and then the solution (7 μL) was dropped on the glassy carbon electrode and dried by infrared as the working electrode; the Silver ion electrode was the reference electrode, where electrolyte was anhydrous acetonitrile solution of 0.1 M TBAP with certain amount of ferrocene as an internal standard using N2 for 0.5 h; the scanning range was −3.5–3 V and the scanning speed was 50 mV s−1. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were calculated from the initial oxidation (

EIS, M−S, i-t, and LSV tests were carried out by attaching the samples to the conductive glass (FTO) through the nafion solution and holding them on the glassy carbon electrode clip; the reference electrode was the Ag/AgCl electrode and the electrolyte was 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. The EIS frequency range was 1–105 Hz; the paranoid voltage of M−S was 0.15 V, and the frequencies were 500, 1000, and 2000 Hz; it used a 300 W xenon lamp as the light source and a 420 nm filter to filter out ultraviolet light; the scanning rate of LSV was 50 mV s−1.

Photocatalytic performance test of ultrapure water. A certain amount of fabricated CZP nanofiber membranes was added into ultrapure water (15 mL) and triethanolamine (1.5 mL) in transparent glass bottles (30 mL). Before the photocatalysis experiment, the bottles with a rubber stopper were sealed using an aluminum plastic cover. Afterward, the reaction was carried out for 6 h under visible light (420 nm ≤ λ ≤ 700 nm) irradiation. After 6 h, the hydrogen content was measured by gas chromatography, and the hydrogen production was calculated by normalizing. After completing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction test, the nanofiber membrane was removed from the glass bottle, rinsed several times with ethanol and ultrapure water, then dried in an oven at 60 ℃. Once fully dried, the membrane was placed into an empty glass bottle, a new solution was prepared, and the sample was exposed to light again to continue the cyclic reactions.

Measurement and calculation of apparent quantum efficiency (AQE). For AQE valuations, the photocatalytic system of ultrapure water (15 mL) and TEOA (1.5 mL) was used, and the illumination area was 9.0746 cm2 under normal temperature and pressure for 6 h. The number of incident photons (N) is calculated by Eq. (3) [31].

The apparent quantum efficiency is calculated from Eq. (4) [32]. In Eq. (4), NA is the molecular number of H2, substituted by Avogadro number (6.02 × 1023).

The microstructure of the ZIS particles was examined using SEM and TEM (Fig. S2). SEM and TEM images reveal that ZIS particles are designed to be less than 10 μm in size, forming flower-like microspheres composed of numerous randomly oriented nanosheets on their surfaces. Subsequently, ZIS particles are incorporated with PAN and CDs to create CZP nanofiber membranes. When comparing the morphology of the CZP nanofiber membranes containing CDs (Fig. S3a–e) with ZIS/PAN nanofiber membranes without CDs (Fig. S3f), no significant fiber agglomeration is observed, and their overall morphology remains similar. Given the relatively poor photocatalytic activity of CDs [33], an excess of particles may hinder the photocatalytic performance of the ZIS component. The fiber diameter distributions reveal that the diameters of CZP nanofibers containing CDs are reduced compared to those of the 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers (Fig. S4). The high conductivity of CDs [34] enhances that of the electrospinning solution, facilitating finer fiber formation during the electrospinning process. This reduction in fiber diameter may also be influenced by aggregated CDs particles. Among various samples, CZP-4 exhibits the smallest average diameter, approximately 200 nm. EDS elemental mapping was performed to ascertain the distribution and composition of the CZP-4 components (Fig. S5). The uniform distribution of Zn, In, and S elements throughout the CZP nanofiber membrane confirms the successful incorporation of ZIS into the composite.

The SEM images of the CZP nanofiber membrane at higher magnification were presented in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a and b illustrate the presence of numerous convex nodules on individual fibers. The increased conductivity of the PAN spinning solution during electrospinning leads to a stronger stretching effect, which facilitates the effective integration of ZIS particles onto individual PAN fibers. Fig. 1a–d demonstrate that ZIS particles are loaded directly onto single fibers, thereby maintaining the availability for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction. High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1e) further confirm the successful incorporation of ZIS and CDs particles on the same fiber. The lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponds to the (102) plane of ZIS, as highlighted by a red dashed square, while the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of CDs [35]. The co-existence of ZIS and CDs particles on the fibers ensures that CDs can act as effective co-catalysts, enhancing the photocatalytic HER activity of ZIS. Elemental mapping (Fig. 1f–i) results confirm that the ZIS particles predominantly consist of Zn, In, and S elements, which are uniformly dispersed across the fiber surface. The successful incorporation of ZIS of the CZP nanofibers is evidenced by the increased nodules.

Fig. 2 a showed the XRD patterns of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane. The diffraction peaks at 21.0°, 27.4°, and 47.5° correspond to the (006), (102), and (110) crystal planes of ZIS, respectively. The relatively low peak intensities are attributed to the low doping concentration of ZIS. The XRD pattern also displays distinct peaks at 16.5° and 29.0°, corresponding to the (100) and (110) crystal planes of the PAN substrate material [36]. The peak near 23.0° observed in the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane is attributed to the glass substrate rather than ZIS or PAN. Additionally, the XRD spectrum of CDs powder exhibits peaks at approximately 22.0° and 39.0°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite [30]. However, no discernible peaks corresponding to CDs are observed in the XRD spectrum of the CZP-4 membrane, likely due to the low concentration of CDs and the overlapping peak of the glass substrate peak around 23.0°. When comparing the XRD spectra of CZP nanofibers with varying CDs concentrations (Fig. S6), it is evident that the overall XRD patterns remain largely unchanged despite different CDs concentrations while maintaining the same ZIS and PAN concentrations. This consistency is due to the minimal amount of CDs added and the weak diffraction intensity.

Simultaneously, FTIR spectra of CZP nanofiber membranes with varying concentrations of CDs were recorded (Fig. S7a). A comparative analysis was performed between the FTIR spectra of CZP-4 nanofibers, ZIS powder, PAN nanofiber membranes, and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofiber membranes (Fig. 2b). In Fig. 2b, the characteristic absorption peaks of PAN within the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane are observed, including the CH stretching vibration at 2931 cm−1, CH bending vibration at 1453 cm−1, CN stretching vibration at 2244 cm−1, NH stretching vibration at 1661 cm−1, CH out-of-plane bending vibrations at 1359 cm−1 and 1229 cm−1, and the CO stretching vibration at 1731 cm−1. These peaks align with the infrared absorption features of 5 % ZIS/PAN and pure PAN nanofiber membranes [37]. As shown in Fig. S7b, it is evident that there is no evident differential FTIR between PAN and 5 % ZIS/PAN. It is consistent with the infrared spectrum of ZIS, with only a –OH peak. The S-In-S-In-S-Zn-S bond in ZIS cannot detect vibration signals due to its inorganic semiconductor properties [38]. For the CDs, the FTIR spectrum shows characteristic peaks at 1720.4 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1586 cm−1 (CC stretching), 1249.8 cm−1 (OCO stretching), and 1022 cm−1 (CO stretching) [39]. In the CZP-4 nanofibers doped with CDs, the CO stretching vibration at 1720.4 cm−1 coincides with that of PAN. This is likely because PAN, being a carbon-based material, exhibits overlapping infrared peak positions with CDs, making it challenging to directly confirm the incorporation of CDs solely based on these peaks. The other characteristic peaks of CDs are weak in CZP-4 and are not prominently detected in 5 % ZIS/PAN and pure PAN nanofiber membranes. The FTIR spectra of CZP nanofibers with different CDs concentrations (Fig. S7) show consistent peak positions but vary in peak intensities. This variance suggests changes in CDs incorporation levels. In the Raman spectra of CDs (Fig. 2c), the D and G bands, which correspond to the disordered and graphitic structures of carbon, are observed at approximately 1352 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1, respectively [33]. For CZP-4, these bands are weak due to the low concentration of CDs. The absence of D and G bands in the spectra of pure PAN and 5 % ZIS/PAN membranes confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the PAN nanofibers. Fig. S8 illustrates the Raman spectra of CZP nanofibers with varying CDs concentrations. It is observed that the D and G bands are not detectable in CZP-1 and CZP-2 but become more pronounced with increasing CDs concentration.

The chemical composition and bonding environment of the surface elements in ZIS powder and CZP-4 nanofiber membranes were further characterized using XPS. As shown in the XPS survey spectra (Fig. S9a), ZIS powder predominantly exhibits peaks for Zn, In, and S, with only trace amounts of C and O, which aligns with the expected. For CZP-4 nanofiber membranes, where PAN serves as the primary material, C, N, and O are the predominant elements observed. Additionally, Zn, In, and S from the incorporated ZIS are detected. The presence of CDs in CZP-4 is inferred rather than directly confirmed by the full spectrum, as the C signals overlap with those from both CDs and PAN. Detailed high-resolution XPS spectra for Zn 2p, In 3d, and S 2p in ZIS powder and CZP-4 were presented in Fig. 2d–f. The Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d) of ZIS powder shows distinct peaks at 1021.9 eV and 1045.0 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [39]. In CZP-4, the Zn 2p peaks are less intense due to the low 5 % loading of ZIS and the comparatively small Zn content in the composite. Notably, the Zn 2p peak in CZP-4 is positively shifted by approximately 0.1 eV compared to ZIS powder. The In 3d spectrum (Fig. 2e) revealed two peaks at 444.8 eV (In 3d5/2) and 452.3 eV (In 3d3/2) for ZIS powder [40]. In CZP-4, the In 3d peaks shift positively by 0.5 eV to 445.3 eV and 452.8 eV, indicating a change in the electronic environment. Similarly, the S 2p spectrum of ZIS powder (Fig. 2f) displays peaks at 161.8 eV and 163.1 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, indicative of the binding energy of S2- [41]. In CZP-4, these peaks shift to 162.4 eV and 163.7 eV, respectively, suggesting significant interaction with the PAN matrix and CDs. In Fig. 2f, a new peak appears at 168.2 eV, corresponding to the higher oxidation state of S atoms. Oxidation is common in the case of hydrothermal synthesis of ZIS [42] and the electrospinning process, likely due to the combined effects of high voltage, ambient air, and elevated humidity levels. The observed positive shifts in the Zn, In, and S peaks upon incorporation into the CZP composite suggest alterations in the binding energies due to the interaction with PAN and CDs. This shift implies a decrease in the electron density around the ZIS surface atoms, facilitating electron transfer from ZIS to CDs and PAN [33,43]. The C 1 s spectrum of CZP-4 (Fig. S9b) reveals main peaks at 284.8 eV and 286.2 eV, corresponding to the CC/CH and CN bonds of PAN, respectively [44]. The peak at 286.2 eV also corresponds to the CO bonds of CDs [28]. Additionally, there are peaks at 288.9 eV attributed to the CO bonds in PAN [44–47] and CDs [30,33], indicating the successful incorporation and interaction of CDs within the composite.

The thermal stability of CZP-4 nanofiber membranes was evaluated using TGA (Fig. S10a). The initial mass loss observed between 50–100 ℃ corresponds to the evaporation of residual solvents and water within the membrane. A minor mass decline in the temperature range of 100–282 ℃ is attributed to the dissociation of weakly bound residual groups and the breakdown of terminal connections such as tail-to-tail or head-to-head bonds. A significant mass reduction occurs between 283–584 ℃, peaking at 318 ℃. This range represents the primary degradation phase, predominantly due to random chain scission, leading to the breakdown of the polymer chains into simpler molecules, which then volatilize at elevated temperatures [48]. As illustrated in Fig. S10b, the residual mass of CZP-4 nanofibers at 800 ℃ is approximately 55.9 %, which is higher than the 53.1 % observed for 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers. This increased residual mass can be attributed to the incorporation of CDs. The presence of CDs likely enhances thermal stability through their adsorption onto the PAN molecular chains and their interaction with PAN during thermal decomposition at high temperatures [49].

Fig. S11a demonstrates that the mechanical properties of CZP-4 nanofibers are significantly enhanced compared to the 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers. The breaking stress of CZP-4 reaches 3.95 MPa, and the elongation at break is 32.4 %. This enhancement is primarily attributed to the incorporation of CDs, which improves the mechanical properties of the nanofibers. CDs possess a surface rich in functional groups, such as benzene rings, which interact with the PAN matrix. PAN contains numerous C, N, and H that readily form hydrogen bonds with CDs. These weak hydrogen bonds tend to break first under stress, absorbing energy and thereby enhancing the mechanical properties of the nanofibers. Additionally, the multifunctional groups on CDs interact with ZIS particles, further contributing to the mechanical strength [50,51]. As shown in Fig. S11b, the mechanical properties of CZP nanofibers initially increase with the addition of CDs and then decrease as the doping concentration continues to rise. This indicates that CZP-4 represents the optimal concentration of CDs. At lower concentrations, CDs are uniformly dispersed within the nanofiber matrix, forming more hydrogen bonds, which enhances the mechanical properties. However, at higher concentrations, CDs tend to agglomerate, leading to uneven dispersion and consequently, a reduction in the mechanical strength of the nanofiber membrane.

Fig. 3 a illustrates that CZP-4 nanofibers exhibit the highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate among all CZP nanofibers, achieving approximately 2250μmol g-1h−1. Fig. 3b demonstrates that PAN nanofibers exhibit negligible photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance, whereas CZP-4 shows the highest hydrogen evolution rate, which is approximately 23 % higher than that of 5 % ZIS/PAN (1836μmol g-1h−1) and 4.55 times higher than that of ZIS powder. Notably, CDs alone exhibit poor photocatalytic performance, producing only about 112μmol g-1h−1. This underscores that the enhanced hydrogen evolution performance of CZP-4 nanofibers due to CDs doping is not merely the sum of CDs and 5 % ZIS/PAN performances; rather, CDs significantly enhance photocatalysis through multiple mechanisms. Furthermore, CZP-5 shows a lower hydrogen evolution rate than CZP-4, indicating that the optimal CDs incorporation level is crucial. Excessive CDs can lead to agglomeration, which affects the light absorption of ZIS particles [33]. Fig. 3c illustrates the hydrogen production cycle stability of CZP-4 nanofiber membranes under visible light for three cycles of 6 h each, demonstrating good photocatalytic stability. Additionally, the cycle stability test underscores the membrane's advantages in terms of easy separation, recycling, and reuse without the need for centrifugation. The material can be directly retrieved, washed with alcohol and water, and dried for subsequent use. Fig. 3d depicts the relationship between hydrogen evolution amount and time for ZIS, 5 % ZIS/PAN, and CZP-4, showing a nearly linear correlation over the tested interval of 6 h. CZP-4 consistently exhibits the highest hydrogen evolution amount at each time point, consistent with the findings in Fig. 3b. H2 evolution performance in nanofiber-based photocatalysts for recent years was summarized in Table S1, providing a high photocatalysis performance compared to others.

From Fig. S12, it can be seen that the XRD spectra of CZP-4 are the same before and after the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction for 6h, indicating that CZP-4 has good cycle stability. The FTIR spectral data of CZP-4 before and after the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction for 6 h were collected in the 400–4000 cm−1 range to investigate the cycle stability of CZP-4. From Fig. S13, it can be seen that the position of peaks did not change significantly, indicating that CZP-4 has good repeatability. The XPS spectra of Zn, In, and S in CZP-4 before and after the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction for 6 h were observed (Fig. S14). There is no essential change, but the intensity of the peaks changes slightly. This is because the ZIS content of CZP-4 is less than 5 %, which is washed after recycling, so the peak intensity becomes weak. Therefore, it can be seen from the XPS spectrum that CZP-4 has good reusability. From the results of XRD, FTIR, and XPS, CZP has good structural stability.

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) analysis, as presented in Fig. 4 a, indicates that although CZP nanofibers exhibit lower light absorption compared to ZIS powder, there is a notable enhancement in absorption within the 200–450 nm range when compared to 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers. This enhancement is attributed to the light-induced electron transfer ability of CDs, which can absorb light, and then emit light at shorter wavelengths as a result of upconversion [52]. The increased absorbance and the red shift of the absorption edge with increasing CDs concentration (Fig. S15) are due to the strong absorption capability of CDs [53], highlighting the substantial contribution of CDs to the light absorption ability of CZP nanofibers. Consequently, the incorporation of CDs can improve the hydrogen evolution performance of the material [35]. In alignment with the UV–Vis DRS data, the hydrogen evolution rate decreased significantly with increasing wavelength, as shown in Fig. S16, and remained highest within the UV region. Beyond 500 nm, the hydrogen evolution rate leveled off, reflecting the diminished light absorption at longer wavelengths. CZP-1 and CZP-4 nanofibers demonstrate an absorption edge near 500 nm, indicating poor light absorption at wavelengths greater than 500 nm. This trend correlates well with the computed apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) values, derived using Eqs. (3) and (4), which are consistent with their UV–Vis DRS profiles (Fig. 4b). The AQE for CZP-4 and 5 % ZIS/PAN at 365 nm is calculated to be 2.39 % and 1.77 %, respectively, underscoring the superior light utilization and efficiency of CZP-4 nanofibers over 5 % ZIS/PAN. The photocurrent density measurements depicted in Fig. S17 reveal that upon illumination with a xenon lamp, all samples exhibit a rapid increase in photocurrent density. Conversely, the photocurrent density diminishes swiftly upon cessation of illumination. CZP-4 exhibited the strongest and most stable photocurrent response among the tested samples. This is attributed to the introduction of CDs, which generate a significant number of photogenerated charge carriers. However, when the concentration of CDs in the fibrous membrane is too high, the CDs tend to aggregate [54]. Therefore, an optimal concentration of CDs is crucial for achieving the best photocurrent response in the fibrous membrane. As a result, CZP-4 demonstrates the best hydrogen evolution performance, capable of producing more hydrogen. Fig. 4c further corroborates this, showing that CZP-4 surpasses both ZIS powder and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofiber membranes in photogenerated current. This enhancement is attributed to the incorporation of CDs, which not only augments the generation of photogenerated electrons but also amplifies the photocurrent response and the overall photocatalytic efficiency. These findings indicate that the integration of CDs into CZP nanofibers enhances their optical properties and photocatalytic performance, by promoting better light absorption and efficient generation and utilization of photogenerated carriers.

According to the DRS test data of CZP nanofibers and Eq. (5) [55], the band gap of CZP-4 is 2.55 eV, respectively (Fig. S18).

In Eq. (5), α is absorbance, h is Planck constant, υ is the frequency, A is a constant, Eg is the semiconductor band gap value, and n is the index, which is related to the semiconductor type. As observed in Fig. S19a, the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane with the best hydrogen evolution effect was characterized by M−S at different frequencies. The positive slope of the M−S curve of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane indicates that it is an n-type semiconductor, which is also consistent with the slopes of the M−S curves of ZIS powder and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofiber membrane. The intersection of the tangent of the M−S curve near 0 V and y = 0 is the flat band potential EFB of the material. The EFB of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane is −1.43 V (vs. NHE, pH=7), and the flat band potential of the n-type semiconductor is close to the bottom of the conduction band in Eq. (6) [56]. Therefore, the conduction band (CB) ECB of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane is −1.63 V (vs. NHE, pH=7), which is less than that of ZIS powder (−1.45 V vs. NHE, pH=7) and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofiber membrane (−1.53 V vs. NHE, pH=7). According to Eq. (7) [57], the valence band (VB) EVB of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane is 0.92 V. Based on the theory, the lower the ECB indicates better electron reduction ability. At pH=7, the conduction band of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane is lower than the reduction potential of H+/H2 (−0.41 V), proving that the material has the ability of hydrogen evolution, which aligns with the results of the photocatalytic test. To determine the band structure of CDs, the CV curve of CDs was shown in Fig. S19b. Through Eq. (1) and (2), the HUMO and LUMO of CDs are −5.97 eV and −3.27 eV (vs. EV), respectively. Accordingly, when pH=7, the HUMO and LUMO of CDs are 1.12 V (vs. NHE, pH=7) and −1.58 V (vs. NHE, pH=7), respectively. Correspondingly, CDs also meet the reduction potential of H+/H2 of −0.41 V (vs. NHE, pH=7).

To comprehensively understand the photocatalytic properties of CDs and CZP-4 nanofiber membranes, we analyze their energy band structure, which includes the band gap values, CB, and VB. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the energy band alignments of CDs, ZIS powder, 5 % ZIS/PAN, and CZP-4 nanofiber membranes are favorable for photocatalytic water splitting. The superior photocatalytic performance of CZP-4 nanofiber membranes compared to ZIS powder and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers can be ascribed to several key factors. Firstly, CZP-4 exhibits an optimally negative reduction potential at its CB position, facilitating reduction processes in photocatalysis. Secondly, the incorporation of CDs narrows the band gap of CZP-4, enhancing its ability to absorb visible light. Thirdly, the efficient electron transition from the VB to the CB in CZP-4 further contributes to its enhanced photocatalytic activity. The mechanism diagram in Fig. 5 e delineates that CDs enhance the optical properties of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane. CDs are known for their excellent light absorbance capabilities. When incorporated into the nanofiber membrane, they significantly boost light absorption, thereby increasing the overall absorbance. Upon exposure to light, several mechanisms contribute to the improved light utilization in CDs-doped CZP-4 nanofiber membranes. The presence of CDs reduces the reflection and transmission of light through the membrane, leading to greater light absorption. This is because CDs can trap more light, thus diminishing the loss of light that would otherwise be reflected or transmitted through the fibers. Moreover, the incorporation of CDs narrows the band gap of the nanofiber membrane, enhancing its optical performance by facilitating the generation of more photogenerated carriers under illumination. These combined effects-enhanced light absorption, reduced reflection and transmission, and a narrowed band gap culminate in the generation of more photogenerated carriers, thereby boosting the overall photocatalytic efficiency of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane.

PL spectroscopy, with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, provided insights into the fluorescence characteristics and the recombination dynamics of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. S20, all CZP nanofibers exhibit emission peaks around 470 nm, corresponding to the bandgap energy of ZIS. Among them, CZP-4 nanofibers exhibit the lowest peak intensity, indicating the weakest fluorescence effect. This observation is attributed to the enhanced interfacial interactions between ZIS and PAN upon the introduction of CDs, which facilitate charge transfer [53]. An optimal concentration of CDs reduces charge recombination, thereby improving charge transfer efficiency. Consequently, CZP-4 demonstrates the lowest electron-hole recombination rate, enhanced charge transfer, and ensures a higher number of electrons are transferred to the surface to participate in reactions, thus improving hydrogen evolution performance. Comparative PL spectra analysis (Fig. 5a) reveals that the fluorescence intensity of CZP-4 is significantly lower than that of both ZIS powder and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers. This implies that the appropriate incorporation of CDs reduces the fluorescence effect and, consequently, the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CZP nanofibers. This reduction is likely attributed to the role of CDs as electron transfer and storage agents. In CZP-4 nanofibers, photogenerated electrons from ZIS are transferred to CDs, where some participate in the reaction with water, thereby enhancing electron-hole separation and extending carrier lifetimes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) further supports these findings. Fig. S21a illustrates that the arc radius of the EIS curve, which indicates photogenerated electron transfer resistance, is the smallest for CZP-4 nanofibers. A smaller arc radius signifies lower photogenerated electrons transfer resistance, which enhances photogenerated electrons transfer efficiency and, consequently, photocatalytic performance [30]. This observation aligns with the PL results and the hydrogen evolution rates. Among the tested samples (Fig. S21b), including ZIS powder, PAN nanofibers, and 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofibers, CZP-4 consistently shows the smallest arc radius, underscoring that the addition of CDs reduces the photogenerated electrons transfer resistance of photogenerated electrons, thereby improving photogenerated electrons transfer and photocatalytic efficiency. TRPL was performed to obtain a quantitative index of photogenerated charge transfer. The TRPL decay curves in Fig. 5b can be fitted (Eq.8) according to the triexponential function in the following equation (τ) [58]:

In Eq. (8), τave and τn are the decay lifetime of luminescence (in nanoseconds), and An is the corresponding weight factor (%). The average PL lifetime (τave) of CZP-4 (16.05 ns) is significantly higher than those of 5 % ZIS/PAN (11.11 ns) and ZIS (13.63 ns). The result indicates that CDs indeed act as charge mediators within the nanofiber membrane, which can capture electrons and promote charge transfer, thereby slowing the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and low electron-hole recombination leads to higher lone pair electron concentration [58]. Therefore, CZP-4 can accelerate charge separation and increase the opportunity for photogenerated electrons to participate in proton reduction for strong photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. The mechanism by which CDs facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is schematically represented in Fig. 5c. ZIS inherently suffers from significant photogenerated electron-hole pairs recombination. Upon illumination, electron-hole pairs are generated on the VB of ZIS, with electrons transitioning to the CB. These electrons can either participate in reactions as active sites or recombine with holes in the VB, leading to a substantial loss of electrons and a decrease in the number of electrons available for continuous reactions. In CDs-doped nanofiber membranes, the presence of CDs significantly alters this dynamic. When ZIS is illuminated, a substantial number of electrons on the CB are transferred to CDs, where they engage in reactions. This effective transfer and storage of electrons by CDs promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, enhancing electron utilization Thus, the incorporation of CDs accelerates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and boosts photocatalytic efficiency by reducing recombination and enhancing charge transfer. It illustrates in detail the strategic incorporation of CDs in CZP-4 nanofiber membranes optimizing the photocatalytic performance through improved charge separation and reduced electron-hole pairs recombination.

With identical ZIS concentrations, the contact angle of the 5 % ZIS/PAN nanofiber membrane was measured at 119° (Fig. S22a). Conversely, CZP-4 nanofiber membranes exhibit a contact angle of 113° (Fig. 6 a), indicating improved hydrophilicity due to CDs incorporation. With increasing CDs concentration (Fig. S25b–e and Fig. 6b), the contact angle of CZP nanofiber membranes gradually decreases, confirming enhanced hydrophilicity. These findings establish CDs as effective hydrophilic agents that improve the wetting properties of CZP nanofiber membranes [59,60]. During photocatalysis, the capillary effect between nanofibers, and the presence of ZIS and CDs particles enable CZP nanofiber membranes to quickly contact with water. This facilitates full immersion of the membrane in water, ensuring thorough contact between internal ZIS and water for effective photocatalytic reactions. The mechanism by which CDs enhance the hydrophilicity of nanofiber membranes is illustrated in Fig. 6c. PAN nanofibers, inherently hydrophobic, tend to shield ZIS particles upon water ingress, creating a hydrophobic environment that hinders water-ZIS interaction and subsequent hydrogen evolution. However, CDs, with their abundant hydrophilic functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2), enhance local hydrophilicity. Upon water penetration, CDs promote deeper infiltration into the fiber structure, facilitating direct contact between water and internal ZIS, thereby promoting photocatalytic water decomposition and hydrogen evolution. This enhancement in hydrophilicity by CDs significantly benefits the photocatalytic performance of the nanofiber membrane.

The mechanism underlying the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution in CZP-4 nanofiber membranes is depicted in Fig. 7 . Upon exposure to visible light, the ZIS within the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane generates photogenerated electron-hole pairs. These photogenerated electrons are excited from the VB to the CB of ZIS, leaving photogenerated holes in the VB. The electrons in the CB participate in the reduction reaction with protons (H+) in water, leading to the generation of hydrogen gas (H2). Concurrently, the holes in the VB are scavenged by the sacrificial agent TEOA, which helps to prevent photo-corrosion of ZIS by capturing the holes and thus, stabilizing the photocatalyst during the reaction.

From the four parts of the HER mechanism, including light absorption, charge separation, electron migration, and surface reaction, this paper deeply expounds the beneficial effect of multifunctional CDs doping on photocatalytic performance. Firstly, because CDs can enhance the light absorption of the material, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs can be generated. Then they can facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs by serving as electron sinks. Most of the electrons generated in the CB are transferred to the CDs. Therefore, CDs can enhance photocatalytic performance through charge separation and electron migration. Finally, CDs significantly improve the hydrophilicity of the membrane, allowing better interaction with water molecules. This enhanced hydrophilicity ensures that the ZIS particles embedded within the membrane are in optimal contact with water. More photogenerated electrons on the surface can contact and react with H+ in water, thus promoting more effective photocatalytic activity. In addition, CDs not only act as efficient electron acceptors but also serve as electron storage sites. The electron storage capability enables the continuous availability of electrons for hydrogen evolution reactions, thereby significantly enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the CZP-4 nanofiber membrane. These synergistic effects of ZIS, PAN, and CDs within the CZP-4 membrane structure lead to heightened photocatalytic activity and improved hydrogen evolution rates.

The photocatalysts integrated into nanofiber membranes increase the feasibility of practical applications, which effectively addresses challenges such as aggregation, deposition, and retrieval of photocatalyst powders in liquid phases. Leveraging the unique properties of CDs mitigates inherent drawbacks of nanofiber membranes in photocatalytic applications, thereby paving the way for scalable and versatile solutions. Furthermore, this research proposes a promising strategy for deviceization through the synergy of nanofibers and photocatalysts. Future investigations could focus on optimizing surface functional groups of CDs, exploring various types, ratios, and concentrations of CDs, and investigating different modes of integrating single fibers or nanofiber membranes with photocatalysts. These endeavors promise to expand the array of options available for photocatalysis, fostering extensive possibilities in integrating nanofiber membranes with photocatalysts to achieve advanced functionalities and device capabilities.

In conclusion, we have explored a multifunctional carbon dots (CDs)-enhanced ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (ZIS/PAN) nanofiber membrane as a highly efficient, reusable, and superior hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) photocatalyst. This material maintains stable dispersion in water and features a convenient recovery process, addressing the challenge of recovering traditional powdered photocatalysts. Additionally, the three-dimensional structured carbon dots/ZnIn2S4/polyacrylonitrile (CZP) nanofiber membrane serves as the carrier for the photocatalyst, minimizing ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) loss during recovery and demonstrating excellent cyclic stability, thereby enhancing practical applicability. The unique structure of the CZP nanofiber membrane combined with multifunctional CDs enhanced the hydrophilicity, increased the light absorption, facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and reduced the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, significantly enhancing HER activity and charge transfer. As a result, the CZP photocatalyst exhibits an optimal photocatalytic performance of 2250 μmol g-1h−1, which is approximately 23 % higher than that of the nanofiber membrane without CDs and 4.55 times greater than that of ZIS particles. Therefore, we have successfully prepared a CDs-enhanced composite photocatalysts based on nanofiber membranes, which has improved the photocatalytic performance compared with the existing photocatalysts [43,61,62]. Thus, this efficient work provides insights into the device fabrication and design flexibility of fiber-photocatalyst composites, highlighting various approaches for practical photocatalytic applications.
